Clark examines Bishop Warburton's Statement that A en. 6.290 provides evidence that Aeneas underwent an initiation. Johnston examines references to the cults of Eleusis, Isis, Dionysus, and Cybele in the Georgics. Setaioli considers the philosophical implications of Vergil's reading of Cicero, div. 1.17-22, traceable in Aen. 7.724ff. and Geo. 1. Whittaker feels that Eel. 4 can in part be understood in light of the influence of the Eleusinian Mysteries on Roman religion.

raw_text: Wehr, Oliver. 2007. "Eine Anspielung auf die Aeneas-Sage in HorazEpode 16?" JAC 22: 131-9. Asztalos defends the transmitted reading "pluma" at Horace, carm. 4.10 on the ground that it alludes to Cycnus at Aen. 10.189-93 and, through this Vergilian filter, to carm. 2.20. Moretti observes Horace's possible allusion to Aen. 11.699-723. Other studies in this section have to do either with intertextuality or with shared themes.

raw_text: Zissos, Andrew. 2008. "Shades of Virgil: Seneca's Troades." MD 61: 191-210. Aiello notes allusions to Vergil and Ovid; Bernstein to Aen. 12.435-40; Bruggisser to Aen. 9.735-7. Ferenczi discusses Valerius Flaccus' use of the Aeneid. Freyburger-Galland shows that Dio Cassius 75.10.2 translates Aen. 11.371-3, and asserts that this passage is Dio's only reference to Vergil. Fucecchi (2007) points to Vergil's influence on Statius, Th. 12.105f., and (2008a) argues that Valerius Flaccus' Amazon at 5.614 is modeled on Camilla in the Aeneid. Gamberale demonstrates that Jerome, epist. 22 alludes to Aen. 3.173 and 10.642. Gärtner includes discussion of the influence of Vergil on Valerius Flaccus. Gioseffi argues that Martial 14.58, followed then by Juvenal 9.102 and Alcuin, carm. 32, alludes to Eel. 2.69; and discusses the reception of Geo. 4.489. Guillaumin notes citation of Aen. 7.234-8 by the gromaticus Siculus Flaccus. Horsfall demonstrates that in at least six instances the text of Lycophron's Alexandra is given point if considered as a variation upon Vergil's Latin. Murgatroyd shows that Valerius alludes to Ovid and to Aen. 8.193-7; and Passarella that Ambrosius, Par. 7.35 alludes to Geo. 4.485-93. Petringa remarks on the influence of Vergil on Cyprian's poetry. Pisano observes Vergilian influence on Apuleius' story of Cupid and Psyche. Santini discusses Aen. 7 as a model for Pun. 13.115-37. Squire notices that allusion to Vergil is made in an inscribed epigra Sperlonga (AE 1967.85).
